text:
For a long time, I have wondered what the difference is between prompt files, custom instructions, and custom agents in ENTITY_036 ENTITY_028 ENTITY_006.

And it's a question I hear a lot. So in this video, we're going to dive in. We're going to take a look at all of those things and how they work together to completely understand when and where to use them. Are you ready? Let's go.

So, before we actually look at custom instructions, prompt files, or custom agents, it's really important for you to understand how the agent system prompt works in ENTITY_036 ENTITY_028 ENTITY_006.

And this is important because you're going to need to know where, in fact, these instructions get inserted into that prompt.

So let's run through that just real quick. If we were to type some sort of a message—just say "hello world" here—and send it to the chat, it gets sent.

But let's take a look at what actually happens behind the scenes here. Behind the scenes, ENTITY_008 composes a prompt, and it starts with a system prompt like this here.

And the system prompt starts off with some core identity and global rules. And this is just very generic stuff.

In fact, I think there's only two or three lines. It's like, "ENTITY_037 are an intelligent AI coding assistant," kind of just this generic identity.

And then underneath that, there are general instructions. These instructions can vary by model because the models have various quirks.

So for instance, if a model is very aggressive about writing the code out to the chat when it should just be writing it to the file, there might be an instruction: "ENTITY_017 print out code blocks with file changes."

ENTITY_002 then underneath that, there are tool-use instructions. These are instructions for the model about how to use the tools that are included in ENTITY_008, like the edit tool, the terminal tool, or the to-do list tool—any of the built-in tools. Underneath that, there are some output format instructions that tell the agent how to format the output for tokenization in the chat so that things like the little file pills that you'll see in ENTITY_036 ENTITY_028 ENTITY_006 actually show up correctly. Now, the next thing that happens is a user prompt gets added to

the message that's sent to the model. It hasn't been sent yet. The user message contains the environment info—information about the user's operating system, etc.—and it also includes workspace info. It literally looks like this: it's your project structure in text format—project name, folders, files. ENTITY_031 another user prompt gets added to the message, which still has not yet been sent; that contains context info like the current date and time, and a list of open terminals that you have. It also contains any files that you have added to the chat. Now, in

our case, we didn't have any files added to the message, but if we were to add files like that, then they would show up right here in this editor context. Finally, we have the "hello world," and then all of this gets sent up to the model. The model responds with this assistant message, and all of these things together are the context window. So the context window is just being built up: we have one, then another one gets added, and then another one gets added here, and then all of this gets sent.

And then the response comes back, and then this gets added. And now this is the whole context window here, right?

All of this. And if we were to add another user message, this would just get added here and would become part of the context.

ENTITY_019. So now you understand what actually happens when you send a prompt. Now the question is: what are custom instructions, prompt files, and custom agents?

And where do they actually go in this prompt? So let's take a look at that. It's probably best if we start with something like custom instructions.

So the most canonical use for custom instructions is to contain high-level information about your project that might help the model do a better job giving you answers.

So for instance, in ENTITY_036 ENTITY_028 ENTITY_006, if you click the gear here, we can generate chat instructions, and this will do exactly what I just said.

It will create an instructions file that has high-level information: your project architecture, and any patterns you can use.

You can actually go and read this prompt here so you can see exactly what it's being asked to write.

But we recommend this for every project. And this is probably the most common use case for custom instructions. Once we've generated these custom instructions, if we were to send another message, you'll see when we do that, the custom instructions automatically get passed right there.

ENTITY_032 they are. So this file is being passed as part of the context. So the question is: where exactly does that show up in the prompt?

So, in our diagram here, custom instructions show up right here in the system prompt, and they're actually added right here.

So they are the last thing in the agent system prompt. It should be noted that the ENTITY_008 instructions will always be the last thing in the agent system prompt because you can create as many instruction files as you like.

So let me give you an example. ENTITY_034 site is called ENTITY_004 ENTITY_008, and it is full of community-contributed prompt files, custom instructions, and custom agents.

It is a treasure trove of custom instructions that you can look at for inspiration. So we could go here and find some instructions that we like.

So here's the table of all the different ones that are available. Let's go down here and let's pick maybe "ENTITY_018.js best practices for LLM."

So if we click install, this will install the file in ENTITY_036 ENTITY_028 ENTITY_006. We can go ahead and accept it.

It asks us where we want to put it. We can put it in the user data folder, which makes it globally available, or we can put it in the .PATH_001 folder, which means it will only be available in this project—which is exactly what I'm going to do.

And now if you look, we'll see we have an instructions folder and a ENTITY_018.js instructions file inside. If we were to pass another prompt here, now you probably guess what's going to happen.

Both of those files actually get passed. And just to clarify, remember that the ENTITY_018.js instructions will always come before the ENTITY_008 instructions file, which always comes last.

So, at a high level, that's custom instructions. Now let's talk about prompt files. ENTITY_024 files are reusable prompts that you can define and then use right in the chat.

Now, why would you do this? Well, let me give you an example. If we want to define a prompt file, we can go to "ENTITY_007 ENTITY_024 ENTITY_010," and you can see that I've got a bunch of different prompt files here.

So let's go ahead and look at this one here, which is called "ENTITY_026." So, I'm going to look at the remember prompt. In the remember prompt, I have a prompt that just builds up a memory file, so I can tell the AI at any time just to remember something, and it will do that. Now you can see up here in the front matter that I can specify the agent; down here, we're just going to stick with the agent, but then we can also add a description. The other very powerful thing here is

that we can also add a model. You can see we get ENTITY_015 for all of the models that we have available; many of these are on ENTITY_020, some of them are built-in. I'm going to use a small model for this so that I don't waste premium requests. What this means is when I use this prompt file, it will automatically move us over to the correct model.

So let me show you what I mean. Suppose the model keeps making the same mistake of trying to use a `useEffect` on server components, which you cannot do in ENTITY_018.js, and so we just want it to remember not to do that.

So we can use that prompt file with just the slash here and then paste in our message. When we send this, you'll notice that we're on ENTITY_005 ENTITY_021 3.5, which is a premium model at 3x, but when we send it, it actually gets sent with GPT-4o because that's what we specified in the prompt file right here. Now it's created an instructions file for us, and this instructions file will be added automatically to every single request.

So you can see here how I'm starting to build up workflows using actually both prompt files and custom instructions.

Now, let's go back to our diagram and take a look at where, actually, in the system prompt these prompt files show up.

So the answer is that they don't; not in the system prompt. ENTITY_033 actually show up down here in the user prompt.

So, in the user prompt, what happens is that these prompt files—their contents—get added right here at the very top.

So even before the context info, we'll have prompt files, and then we're going to have just the contents of the prompt file that was used.

And then what happens is down here in this message, it says, "ENTITY_011 the instructions in," and then it points back up to the contents of that prompt file.

But it actually uses a special syntax to do that. It's pointing to it by name, even though it's actually included in the user prompt. After that, it has your system message, which would be "ENTITY_035 effects can only be used in client components," right? So that's where prompt files come in. If we have a lot of messages, they may be way down in the list, right? Maybe way down here. Each time you use a prompt file, it becomes part of the user prompt, not part of the system prompt.

Now, the question that you're going to ask is: does the placement of the prompt within the message hierarchy actually matter?

The answer to that is, I don't know. However, context rot is a real thing. The basic idea here is that as the context window grows and gets longer, the performance of the model degrades.

Now, this has gotten better over time, as the article dives into here. It's improved, but you can still see here that as the context window grows—for example, a 32,000-token prompt—accuracy drops dramatically.

Even ENTITY_005 3.5 ENTITY_027 goes from 88% to 30% accuracy. So it's important to remember that as your prompt is growing here—if we have a system prompt, a user prompt, an assistant message, another user prompt, and then another assistant message—it's because we haven't started a new chat.

So it just keeps going and going. It doesn't really matter if you're using custom instructions or prompt files; the performance or the accuracy of the model is just going to degrade.

And this is one of the reasons that the token windows or the context windows in VS ENTITY_006 are limited at a certain point; that is to maintain performance.

So it's hard to answer the question of whether you would be more accurate passing your instructions as a prompt file or a custom instruction. The best thing to do, instead of worrying about the positioning, is just use them as they are designed, which is to help you compose workflows. Now let's talk about one last thing: custom agents. ENTITY_009 agents used to be called custom modes, and I built one a while back called "beast mode" that was designed to help GPT-4 perform better. The idea here is that you can pass

instructions to sort of override or augment the default agent behavior. Let's take a look at these; I want to look at one of the ones that we're now shipping in ENTITY_036 ENTITY_028 ENTITY_006, which is the ENTITY_022 mode. You can actually click on "ENTITY_007 ENTITY_009 ENTITY_001" and look at our built-in ENTITY_022 mode here. Let's take a look at the ENTITY_022 mode. You can see that in the name and the description, then there's these tools, and then there's these things called handoffs, which we'll get to in just a second.

You can see that it's very much like we're writing an agent prompt. ENTITY_034 is very different from custom instructions, right? If we look at the custom instructions, those just give information, while this gives an identity. It's very much like an agent system prompt. It uses a workflow to start a planning process with the user: the first step is to gather context and research, then present a concise plan for iteration, and then handle user feedback. Finally, it will give you the option to either implement the plan or write it out to

the editor, and it does that via handoffs. So let's actually run this and take a look at what this looks like in action.

I'm going to pick a model that's a little bit better at planning here; I'm using our built-in ENTITY_022 mode.

So let's do something that is always done in these demos: we're just going to add dark mode to an app.

I don't know why that's the prompt that's always used, but it's because it's visual and something we can see.

So we're going to let ENTITY_012 go through and work through this plan—you can see this—and then we'll come back and talk about what is actually happening here.

ENTITY_019, so we're using this new ENTITY_022 agent and we've sent this prompt. What is actually happening behind the scenes?

Let's go back and take a look at our diagram. So, what happens when you use a custom agent is this: it actually gets added to the system prompt here.

Let's make some more room. The custom agent is always added here, below your custom instructions. ENTITY_029's the order in the system prompt: custom instructions will be added to the end of the system prompt, and then the custom agent instructions are added to the end of those.

ENTITY_033're the last thing in the agent system prompt. So now let's take a look at how we can use custom instructions, prompt files, and custom agents to compose agentic workflows, because that's what they are.

ENTITY_032 isn't really a right or wrong way to use them. ENTITY_033're just building blocks for composing your own workflows, but that doesn't really make any sense in the abstract. It's going to help if you see how someone else has done it. I want to show you how I compose workflows. I'm going to use a prompt file for planning—I have a custom planning prompt file—and then I'm going to ask it to refactor the UI of this application to be more clean and modern. You'll notice the first

thing that it does is switch us over to ENTITY_021 4.5 because that's what I've defined in the prompt file. We can actually take a look at that if we go here. ENTITY_013 is my prompt file; you can see I've defined the model. My prompt file for planning is very similar to the built-in agent, but it actually instructs the agent to work in the concept of a branch. So the plan is really defining one PR or one branch, and it's just defining the high-level steps. It's going to do things that the built-in ENTITY_022 custom agent does,

like researching the codebase and asking me questions.Но the plan format that it spits out is a little bit different because this is just one of the building blocks; it'll make more sense here in a second.

ENTITY_019, so planning mode is done and we have one file here that's been created. I have it create the file automatically.

It does have some questions, but they're mild—not anything that I really need to address. You can see it just breaks things down into steps.

Step one: do this. Step two: do this. The idea is that each one of these steps is a commit, and commits should be small and testable. Commits will build up and create a single PR that we can then submit.

We don't have any code though. So the next step is to actually get code. Let's go ahead and keep this, and because we don't want context rot, let's start a new chat session here.

Clear the context. Now what I'm going to do is something a little bit unique. Instead of actually implementing the plan, I am going to have it generate a document based on that plan.

And then I'm just going to pass the plan file here. In this case, I'm using a generate prompt and passing in the plan that we just created.

I'll explain what it's doing. The generate prompt takes the plan and then it writes all of the code required to implement it, but it doesn't write that code in the project. It actually writes it in a markdown file step-by-step. The reason I'm doing this is because I'm trying to maximize my premium model usage. I've used ENTITY_005 ENTITY_021 4.5 twice now. It is a 3x multiplier and has six premium requests.

I want to make sure that I'm getting the most bang for my buck, so I'm actually going to use a smaller model to implement and a bigger model to write the code. The smaller model will implement it, but the bigger model writes it. So in just a second we'll take a look at this implementation plan, and you can see exactly how this works. It's not complicated; the implementation plan that gets generated is long — it's very long, this one's almost 2,000 lines long — but you can see that every single piece of code that is needed to accomplish this job is actually in this file. Important here is that they're all broken up into steps, and each step has a checkbox here. So now that we have that, we're going to clear the context window again, making the best use of our context, and now we're going to use a custom agent which is just called "ENTITY_014". All we have to do is then pass in the implementation plan here, like this, and give it a simple prompt. ENTITY_031 we just send it, and you can see it automatically moves to the VS ENTITY_006 ENTITY_023 or ENTITY_025 ENTITY_023, which is a ENTITY_016 variant, and it's the model...

ENTITY_029 I like to use for implementation. So we'll go ahead and send that off. Now, as this smaller model works on this document, it's going to implement what's in the document just verbatim. It isn't actually writing any code; it's just implementing the code that the large model wrote. ENTITY_034 strategy lets you sort of "one-shot" with a huge model and then implement and iterate with a small model. ENTITY_034 model will keep going until it's completed whatever step it's on in the implementation plan, and then it will stop and return control to me so that I can...

Test and make sure that I like it. ENTITY_031 I will just stage and commit, and then I will just redo what I did: use the implement agent, pass the implementation document, and it will just pick up with step two and continue. I will just iterate with it like that until we get to the end of the implementation and everything's working. ENTITY_031 I have a pull request that I feel really good about. ENTITY_019, so now you have a really good understanding of how things work behind the scenes for the agent in VS ENTITY_006. You understand the agent system prompt and how the user prompts...

Are added on and where custom instructions and prompt files actually go in that prompt. Go forth and create AI workflows that work for you. I'll put links to mine below. Check out the ENTITY_004 ENTITY_008 repo, where you'll find my workflows plus tons of other prompt files, instructions, and custom agents that you can use today. And as always, happy coding.
